coredata.dc:description: The activity of photocatalytic oxygen reduction to produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is still severely limited by its slow charge dynamics. Compared to previous work that focused on promoting electron-hole separation, this study emphasizes a strategy to optimize H2O2 photosynthesis by enhancing proton supply. The amino polycarboxylic acid (APCA) chelating agent was connected to the surface of a novel carbon-based photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), resulting in an increase in H2O2 production rate from 512 to 2200 μmol g−1h−1. Impressively, the prepared CQDs can directly capture oxygen from the air, increasing the dissolved oxygen content in solution by 40 %. Air was used as a sustainable oxidant in energy conversion. Mechanism research found that the incorporation of APCA caused a negative shift in the conduction band position of CQDs, and the amide bonds formed on the catalyst acted as a proton relay. The accelerated proton transport promoted the separation and transfer of electrons, and enhanced the continuous single electron reduction of oxygen through proton coupled electron transfer reactions, with a selectivity of up to 94 % for H2O2 production. This study proposes a simple and excellent functional group mediated proton transfer enhancement method as a new choice for high-performance H2O2 green production.

head.ce:title: Construction of proton relay on carbon quantum dots for highly efficient hydrogen peroxide photo-production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The activity of photocatalytic oxygen reduction to produce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is still severely limited by its slow charge dynamics. Compared to previous work that focused on promoting electron-hole separation, this study emphasizes a strategy to optimize H2O2 photosynthesis by enhancing proton supply. The amino polycarboxylic acid (APCA) chelating agent was connected to the surface of a novel carbon-based photocatalyst, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), resulting in an increase in H2O2 production rate from 512 to 2200 μmol g−1h−1. Impressively, the prepared CQDs can directly capture oxygen from the air, increasing the dissolved oxygen content in solution by 40 %. Air was used as a sustainable oxidant in energy conversion. Mechanism research found that the incorporation of APCA caused a negative shift in the conduction band position of CQDs, and the amide bonds formed on the catalyst acted as a proton relay. The accelerated proton transport promoted the separation and transfer of electrons, and enhanced the continuous single electron reduction of oxygen through proton coupled electron transfer reactions, with a selectivity of up to 94 % for H2O2 production. This study proposes a simple and excellent functional group mediated proton transfer enhancement method as a new choice for high-performance H2O2 green production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as a green and efficient energy medium, has broad prospects in energy storage and conversion [1]. Its oxidative property is also widely used in pollution control, organic synthesis, and other fields [2]. It is considered an ideal resource for solving environmental problems and energy crises. At present, the industrial production of H2O2 mainly relies the anthraquinone (AQ) process, which requires precious metal catalysts, hydrogen gas, and high-temperature and high-pressure environments, posing serious limitations in terms of cost and safety [3]. Therefore, exploring its in-situ efficient production technology has important research value and practical significance.

Photocatalytic H2O2 production (PHP) utilizes semiconductor catalysts to capture light to produce H2O2 [4]. As an energy-saving, environmentally friendly, and sustainable method, it provides a more attractive route for H2O2 synthesis and is considered one of the most promising green synthesis methods for H2O2, with broad research prospects [5–7]. The use of solar energy can generate H2O2 through oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) by photogenerated electron (Eq. 1) and water oxidation reaction (WOR) by photogenerated hole (Eq. 2) [8]. Due to the higher overpotential required for generating H2O2 (1.76 V vs. NHE) from WOR compared to oxygen evolution reaction (1.23 V vs. NHE) (Eq. 3), PHP mainly originates from the two-electron ORR pathway [9]. The photocatalytic ORR can also generate H2O through a four-electron process (Eq. 4), which reduces the selectivity for H2O2 production [10,11].
(1) O2 + 2e− + 2H+ → H2O2
(2) 2H2O + 2 h+ → H2O2 + 2H+
(3) 2H2O + 4 h+ → O2 + 4H+
(4) O2 + 4H+ + 4e− → 2H2O

In recent years, various photocatalysts have been developed for catalyzing the production of H2O2 through ORR, including metal oxides [12], metal organic frameworks [13] or coordination polymers [14], and graphite carbonitride (g-C3N4) [4,15,16]. The main efforts to improve PHP efficiency often focus on improving charge separation. A large amount of research works have provided various strategies, such as forming heterojunctions [17], defect engineering [18], element doping [19], morphology control [20], etc., to enhance the electron separation and transport of photocatalysts. Actually, ORR involves proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) steps and multiple reaction pathways, in which, in addition to electrons, interfacial proton activity is also the core of PCET kinetics and ORR performance [21–23]. Due to the slow kinetics of WOR (in seconds), it is difficult to act as a proton source for faster ORR (in microseconds to milliseconds) [24–26]. Protons often need to be provided through the oxidation of organic sacrificial agents (such as methanol, ethanol, benzyl alcohol), which can easily lead to difficulties in subsequent H2O2 separation [27,28]. Currently, there are few studies exploring effective methods to replace sacrificial agents from the perspective of promoting proton extraction and efficient transfer. This is the key step in PHP, and may also be an important breakthrough in improving the production efficiency of H2O2.

As a new type of carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with excellent photo-response performance have been proven to serve as effective electron acceptors/donors to enhance the photocatalytic/electrocatalytic efficiency of many semiconductor materials, such as TiO2, ZnO, Fe3O4, CuWO4, CdS, Ni4P2, and C3N4 [29–34]. CQDs themselves also have suitable conduction band position that enable them to catalyze ORR, making them a new choice for high-performance PHP. Moreover, it is worth noting that CQDs have sp2 graphitized domains, and the hybridization of O 2p orbitals and π orbitals may lead to oxygen bonding with graphite carbon [35,36]. Compared with most photocatalysts with poor surface oxygen absorption performance, CQDs have the potential to use air as a sustained oxidant by promoting the dissolution of oxygen [37,38]. Recently, studies have showed that some intrinsic functional groups on the surface of CQDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, can provide protons for the ORR [39–41]. However, these studies still mainly aim to enhance photocatalytic performance from the perspective of promoting photo-induced carrier separation. For example, Gu et al. [42] increased the H2O2 production from 609.4 to 1187.8 μmol g−1h−1 with anthraquinone functionalized CQDs, in which anthraquinone is considered a co-catalyst that promotes charge separation and two-electron selectivity in PHP through sequential hydrogenation and dehydrogenation.

ORR is the PCET reaction involving Brønsted acid-base pairs. Proton relay is an acid-base site located in the second coordination sphere of the catalyst, which can improve the efficiency of proton transfer [43]. In previous studies, by coordinating the central metal and using the functional groups on ligands (such as porphyrin and carbazole) as proton relays, it has been found that efficient proton transfer can activate O-O bond and improve ORR [44]. Turro et al. [45] demonstrated that the N-H bond on the bncn (benzo[c]cinnoline) ligand of bimetallic electrocatalyst can play a proton relay role and significantly increase the local proton concentration near the catalytic active center, which is an effective method to enhance the hydrogen evolution reaction activity. Liao et al. [46] found that there are active protons at the uncoordinated nitrogen atom in the CuBtz structure based on copper ions and 1H benzotriazole ligands, which can serve as a proton source for CO2 electroreduction. Obtaining protons from proton relay has a lower energy barrier than directly obtaining protons from the electrolyte, and protons can be supplemented from the electrolyte to promote efficient conversion of CO2 to C2+ products.

At present, there have been no reports on the application of proton relays in carbon-based PHP. Considering the non-metallic nature and large number of surface functional groups of CQDs, this study will explore the installation of proton relay on CQDs to ensure stable and efficient proton transfer on inorganic photocatalyst. The currently discovered proton conductive inorganic and organic materials mainly include hydrate type, hydrogen bonding type, hydrogen embedding type, lattice defect type, conductive polymer, etc. [47–50] The amino polycarboxylic acid (APCA) chelating agents commonly used in pharmaceuticals, daily necessities, and food, such as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), have a structure where several carboxyl groups are connected by one or more N atoms, providing the conditions for proton supply. Considering that amide groups are a component of many conductive polymers, surface amino groups on CQDs can undergo amidation reactions with the carboxyl groups of APCA, which may achieve controllable, stable, and efficient connection of APCA while possessing good proton conductivity. Jiang et al. [51] used amide bonds to connect monomer, the obtained polymer-CQDs membranes with proton conductivity exceeding 100 mS cm−1. Through amidation rection, the π electrons in the carbonyl group can form P-π conjugation with the lone electrons on the nitrogen atom, leading to a decrease in its electron cloud density and an increase in the polarity of the N-H bond. [52,53] Therefore, the amide bonds are more prone to deprotonation. In addition, under weakly acidic conditions, the hydrogen of the remaining carboxyl groups in APCA will transfer to the N atom to form a bipolar ion, and the proton will be taken from the solution to maintain its form. That is to say, the amide groups constructed on the surface of CQDs can serve as proton relay to continuously provide protons, triggering electron transfer through sufficient proton supply, thereby improving the efficiency of ORR.

Enhancing proton transfer represents a novel approach to develop efficient PHP, which may prevent the potential accumulation of electrons and energy loss, thereby achieving the optimization of thermodynamic driving force in consecutive reaction [54,55]. This study aims to (i) construct protonated functional groups on the surface of CQDs to verify their ability as proton relay; (ii) explore the oxygen enrichment and photocatalytic H2O2 production performance of CQDs under accelerated proton transfer; (iii) analyze the pathway and mechanism of H2O2 production by CQDs with proton relay. In this work, proton relay was constructed on non-metallic carbon-based photocatalyst using common chelating agents and simple amidation method to enhance proton transfer and then promote PCET, achieving efficient H2O2 production through ORR. Directly using air as a cheap oxidant makes this process highly environmentally adaptable and has enormous potential in practical applications.

Citric acid, potassium titanium oxalate, and deuteroxide were supplied by Tianjin Sanjiang Sairuida Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Urea, ethylene glycol, and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), nitrilotriacetic acid (NTA), ethylenediamine-N, N’-disuccinic acid (EDDS), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) were provided by Tianjin Solomon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All the reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification.

CQDs were synthesized by glycol solvothermal method. Compared with hydrothermal method, the interaction between solvent ethylene glycol molecules and CQDs can optimize the surface state of CQDs, thereby improving the fluorescence emission efficiency. In addition, this method can improve the temperature and pH stability of CQDs, and provide abundant surface oxygen-containing functional groups for CQDs. Using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source, citric acid ions react with the amino groups in urea to undergo deamination reaction, producing ammonia gas and intermediate products, which further react to form graphene structure. The graphene structure is completely aromatic, which leads to the formation of CQDs. 0.615 g citric acid and 0.385 g urea were completely dissolved in 30 ml glycol under continuously magnetic stirrring for 2 h. The resulting solution was then transferred into a 50 ml PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 7 h[56,57]. The obtained black solid product was subsequently filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate larger particles, followed by dialysis in DI water for 24 h (MWCO 1000) to remove reactant precursors and smaller CQDs. Upon completion of dialysis, the black-brown powdered CQDs were obtained after freeze-drying for approximately 30 h.

The carboxyl group of EDTA was linked to the amino group of CQDs through amidation reaction. CQDs synthesized from urea usually contain amino groups on their surface. EDTA is a polydentate ligand containing multiple carboxyl groups. Under the action of catalyst EDC and condensation agent NHS, the carboxyl group of EDTA is activated by EDC to form an intermediate, and the amino group on CQDs acts as a nucleophile to attack the activated carboxyl carbon atom and form a transition state complex. Water molecules are removed from the transition state complex to form an amide bond, obtaining the amidation product connecting CQDs and EDTA. The NHS is often used in conjunction with EDC to increase the reaction efficiency and stability. NHS can react with the intermediates formed by EDC to generate more stable activated esters, which then react with amino groups to form stable amide bonds, thereby improving the reaction yield [58,59]. Typically, 25 mg of CQDs powder was added to 25 mL of MES buffer (pH = 6), sonicated for 20 min, and then purged with nitrogen for 30 min to remove dissolved oxygen from the solution. The solution was then placed in an ice bath, and 100 mg of EDC and 50 mg of NHS were added successively. Subsequently, different doses of EDTA were added (8 mg, 12.5 mg, 25 mg, 50 mg, 75 mg), and a small amount of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was slowly added to the above solution to adjust the pH to 7.4. The mixture was then continuously stirred under a nitrogen atmosphere for 24 h to ensure complete reaction between EDTA and CQDs. After the reaction, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h to remove unreacted materials and buffer solution. After dialysis, the EDTA-CQDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying.

The photocatalytic production of H2O2 was conducted using a 300-W Xenon Arc lamp as the light source. The reaction system was not aerated, in direct contacted with air and the catalyst concentration is 0.2 g/L. Dissolved oxygen and pH were measured in 50 mL of solution. In the control group, 10 vol% ethanol was used as the sacrificial agent. Prior to illumination, the solution was stirred in the dark for 30 min. Subsequently, samples were collected every 5 min using a syringe. The production of H2O2 was quantitatively determined using the potassium titanium oxalate method (400 nm, ε = 43600

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to determine the phase of prepared materials by using an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The main surface chemical elements were characterized by Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo, America). The microstructure and appearance of materials were observed on a JEM-2800 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a TENSOR 37 FTIR spectrometer (BRUKER, Germany) using KBr pellets. The UV–visible diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was recorded on a U4150 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed utilizing a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was measured using a FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). The reactive oxidative species (ROS) were identified via an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany).

Electrochemical experiments were conducted in a standard three-electrode system and measured with an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, China). The detection system employed a 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution (pH = 7) as the electrolyte, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode (Pt). Ten milligrams of catalyst were dispersed in a solution containing 600 μL of water, 300 μL of ethanol, and 100 μL of Nafion (5 wt%), and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min to form a catalyst ink. The prepared catalyst ink was then drop-cast onto the rotating ring-disk electrode and allowed to dry at room temperature.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out at a scanning rate of 0.1 V /s in the potential range of −0.8 V ∼ 0.4 V versus Ag/AgCl to analyze and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Tafel plots of the catalyst. The geometric area of GCE was 7.1 mm2. Rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurements were performed at the same scanning rate (0.1 V/s) and different rotation speeds of 900 rpm and 1600 rpm, and the disk potential was swept from −1.4 V to 0.4 V versus Ag/AgCl, while maintaining a constant Pt ring voltage to fully oxidize the H2O2 generated on the disk electrode. TEM analysis showed that both CQDs and EDTA-CQDs have a nearly spherical appearance (Fig. 1
a, b), and the average diameters were calculated to be 4.8 ± 1.3 nm and 9.2 ± 2.1 nm respectively (Fig. 1 c), indicating the combination with EDTA increases the size of catalyst. XRD patterns of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs ( Fig. S2 ) exhibit characteristic peaks at 26°, corresponding to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon. HRTEM image of CQDs (Fig. 1 d) also shows hexagonal lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which matches well with the (100) plane of graphite carbon (Text S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and oxygen enrichment performance of EDTA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The changes in light absorption and carrier recombination of EDTA-CQDs were determined through UV/visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy ( Fig. S3 ). EDTA-CQDs exhibit characteristic absorption peaks at 230 nm (π-π* transition of sp2-carbon lattice) and 393.5 nm (n-π * transition of CO), with a blue shift indicating an enlarged bandgap. The introduction of EDTA enhances the light absorption in both the ultraviolet and visible light regions. The excitation wavelength of the characteristic PL spectrum is determined by the UV absorption wavelength, and the maximum emission wavelength position of EDTA-CQDs and CQDs were essentially consistent. Combined with the three-dimensional fluorescence matrix scanning images (Fig. 3
a, b), the excitation light intensity of EDTA-CQDs was significantly reduced over the entire wavelength range, indicating the inhibition of photoexcited carrier recombination. The average PL lifetimes of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs were calculated to be 1.74 ns and 1.81 ns respectively through fitting with a biexponential function (Fig. 3 c, Text S2). From the perspective of charge separation kinetics, the PL lifetime is inversely proportional to the electron-hole recombination rate. A longer PL lifetime indicates a lower recombination rate and higher migration rate of photo-generated carriers. EDTA-CQDs exhibit slower exponential decay kinetics, suggesting an accelerated transfer of electrons in EDTA-CQDs.

The bandgap energy (Eg) and valence band (VB) levels of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs were determined and calculated using UV/visible absorption spectroscopy and XPS valence band spectra (Fig. 3 d, e). From the VB-XPS plots, the VB potential of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs was measured as 2.01 and 1.67 eV, respectively. According to the formula in the literatures [65,66], the VB value of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs was calculated to be 2.12 and 1.78 eV, respectively. The band gap results revealed that the Eg (2.71 eV) of EDTA-CQDs increased compared with CQDs (2.22 eV), corresponding to the slightly blue shift in peak positions observed in the UV/visible absorption spectrum ( Fig. S3 ). Obviously, both VB and CB positions shifted negatively after connecting EDTA (Fig. 3 f). The negative shift of CB edge will promote the continuous two-step single electron ORR of H2O2 production [67].

The solubility and diffusion rate of oxygen in water are relatively low. Although pre-saturation or continuous aeration can increase the reaction rate, the high cost of these operations hinders the development of highly active photocatalysts. In this study, after using nitrogen to remove oxygen from water and adding photocatalysts, it was observed that CQDs can spontaneously absorb oxygen from the air, increasing the dissolved oxygen (DO) content in the solution. In a dark environment, the change in DO content over time (Fig. 4
a) reveals that the oxygen dissolution rate (rate constant of zero order reaction: 1.7 mg/Lh−1) in CQDs aqueous solution is significantly accelerated, which reaches 2.4 times that (0.7 mg/Lh−1) of pure water in the initial stage (within 5 h), and the saturated DO content increases by 40.3 %, indicating that CQDs exhibit an oxygen enrichment effect. Under illumination, the DO content in the solution decreases by 11.1 %, suggesting that some DO in water is rapidly consumed. Due to the continuous replenishment of oxygen in the air into the reaction system under the effect of oxygen enrichment, the homeostasis oxygen content in the solution under illumination is still higher than that in water. The saturated DO content of EDTA-CQDs system (333.1 μM) is not significantly different from that of CQD system (331.9 μM), indicating that the connection of surface EDTA does not alter the oxygen enrichment performance of CQDs. The intrinsic functional groups of CQDs determine their unique oxygen adsorption ability. It has been observed that the adsorption energy of oxygen is negative in the presence of CO group on carbon quantum dots but positive for other functional groups, such as –COOH and –OH [37]. The modification process in this study did not significantly affect the main oxygen adsorption sites. After illumination, the DO content in EDTA-CQDs system decreased by 27.9 %, which is significantly lower than that in CQD system, indicating that the DO in water was consumed more rapidly by EDTA-CQDs. This corresponds to the significant increase of H2O2 production in the EDTA-CQDs system. In addition, the cycling tests of DO level in CQDs and EDTA-CQDs aqueous solution (Fig. 4 b) show that the DO content in the solution can recover to its original level within a day in the dark after the photocatalytic reaction consumes some oxygen in the solution. Unlike conventional photocatalysts, CQDs, which effectively and sustainably absorb oxygen directly from the air, provide sufficient conditions for ORR to produce H2O2 without the need for aeration, saving on energy costs. This is because, similar to graphene, the graphitized carbon of CQDs can bond with molecular oxygen, and the oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs (e.g. CO) can interact and oxygen, thus altering the adsorption energy of O2 molecule. [37,38].

By altering the mass ratio of precursors (EDTA:CQDs = 1:3, 1:2, 1:1, 2:1 and 3:1) involved in the amidation reaction, the utilization rate of carboxyl groups of EDTA can be controlled. The results show that the highest photocatalytic efficiency of EDTA-CQDs is achieved when the mass ratio of EDTA to CQDs was 1:1, reaching 2200 μmol g−1h−1 (Fig. 5 c, d). More EDTA can provide sufficient carboxyl groups to react with amino groups on the surface of CQDs to form amide bonds as the possible proton donors. But excessive EDTA can also cover the active surface of CQDs, affecting the mass transfer and reaction between CQDs and O2. At the ratio of 1:1, the SCC efficiency (η) of EDTA-CQDs under illumination was calculated to be 0.21 %, which is much higher than that of CQDs (0.049 %), and at a relatively high level compared with the reported literatures [42].

The enhanced performance of EDTA-CQDs in catalyzing the production of H2O2 was also verified through electrochemical analysis. To compare the electrochemical kinetics of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs in ORR, a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) was used to measure the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves at rotational speeds of 900 rpm and 1600 rpm (Fig. 6
a). Compared to CQDs, the ORR set-on potential of EDTA-CQDs significantly shifts negatively, indicating EDTA-CQDs have higher ORR activity. The ring and disk current density of EDTA-CQDs are both higher than those of CQDs. By calculating the current density at different stable rotation speeds within the potential range of −1.4 V to −0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), the average electron transfer number of CQDs is 2.31, with an 84.02 % selectivity for generating H2O2. The average electron transfer number of EDTA-CQDs decreases to 2.12, which is closer to the ideal two-electron ORR, and the selectivity for H2O2 production in the oxygen reduction reaction rises to 94.05 % (Fig. 6 b). The high selectivity towards H2O2 may be due to the modification of the N-CQDs by the amidation reaction, which increases the ratio of pyrrolic-N to pyridinic-N in EDTA-CQDs (CQDs: 2.44, EDTA-CQDs: 3.03) ( Fig. 2 c, d). It has been reported that the increase in the relative content of pyrrolic-N can weaken the four-electron ORR, which is conducive to the synthesis of H2O2 [70,71]. This is also consistent with the previous band calculation results.

Tafel polarization curves show that the corrosion potential of EDTA-CQDs is lower, indicating that functionalization improves the reactivity of CQDs, making the oxygen reduction reaction more likely to occur (Fig. 6 c). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements also reveal a decrease in charge transfer resistance (R ct) from 2049 Ω to 1870 Ω after combination (Fig. 6 d). The calculated proton conductivity [72] of CQDs and EDTA-CQDs is 1.37 × 10−4 S cm−1 and 1.51 × 10−4 S cm−1, respectively. It can be seen that the proton conductivity is improved after amidation connection. These electrochemical test results suggest that constructing proton relay on CQD provides a more effective catalyst for the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 by enhancing charge transfer and promoting selectivity for the two-electron ORR.

In order to elucidate the reaction pathways of the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 by EDTA-CQDs, radical quenching experiments were first conducted to preliminarily identify the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the reaction process. P-benzoquinone (p-BQ), L-histidine (L-His) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were used as scavengers for superoxide radicals (·O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), and photo-generated holes (h+) respectively. The results show that·O2 − and 1O2 are both critical reactive species in the photocatalytic process of EDTA-CQDs, and h+ also play a certain role in the conversion of reactive species (Fig. 7 a). According to the band structure, the CB potential of EDTA-CQDs (−0.93 eV) is lower than the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV), indicating that a photoexcited electron (Eq. 9) of the catalyst can react with O2 to generate ·O2 − (Eq. 10). Another electron and a proton transfer to ·O2 − to generate HO2· (Eq. 11), which then reacts with another proton to generate H2O2 (Eq. 12). When adding p-BQ, the yield of H2O2 decreases by 91.5 %, indicating that ·O2 − is the main intermediate for generating H2O2, and the continuous single-electron reaction pathway is dominant in the PHP process. The importance of ·O2 − is also reflected in its role as a precursor for the formation of other ROS, such as 1O2. The major function of photogenerated h+ is to oxidize ·O2 − to 1O2 (Eq. 13), which is then further transformed to H2O2 through PCET [73] (Eqs. 12 and 14).

EPR spin trapping technology was used to further detect the active species in the system. As shown in the Fig. 7b, compared with CQDs, the signal intensity of photogenerated h+ and peroxyl radicals (HO2·) in the EDTA-CQDs system is significantly higher, confirming that the functionalized CQDs produce more h+ and HO2·, which is consistent with the significant increase in ORR rate. Furthermore, to analyze the transformation process of 1O2, which was detected after trapping O2 − by p-BQ (Fig. 7 c, d). It was found that the EPR signal of 1O2 significantly decreased, but did not completely disappear. This indicates that in addition to the electron transfer mechanism, 1O2 may also be produced from O2 through the energy transfer from the excited photocatalyst to the ground state O2 (triplet state oxygen, 3O2) (Eq. 15) [74]. When 2,4-hexadienoic acid (HDA) is used as a triplet capture agent, the productions of 1O2 slight decrease in both CQDs and EDTA-CQDs systems, indicating that 3O2 is also a precursor to the formation of 1O2. It can be seen that CQDs can achieve the transition of singlet excitons to triplet states and spin flipping of oxygen to produce 1O2. There is a lower 1O2 content in the EDTA-CQDs system than in the CQDs system, suggesting that more 1O2 is converted into HO2· through accelerated PCET, which is consistent with the higher HO2· content of EDTA-CQDs in Fig. 7b.

Based on the above analysis, EDTA-CQDs mainly generate H2O2 through two-step single electron ORR. Part of ·O2 − is not directly translated to H2O2, but to 1O2. The converted·O2 − also contributes to the formation of H2O2. It is deduced that the main PHP reactions involved in the EDTA-CQDs system are as follows:
(9) EDTA-CQDs + hν → EDTA-CQDs* + h++e−
(10) EDTA-CQDs* + O2 + e− → EDTA-CQDs+·O2 −
(11) ·O2 −+e−+H+ → HO2·
(12) HO2·+H+ → H2O2
(13) ·O2 −+h+ → 1O2
(14)
1O2 + H++e− → HO2·.
(15) O2 + triplet state (3O2) → 1O2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proton relay effect of EDTA-CQDs in PHP
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, in-situ FT-IR was used to study the changes of the surface functional groups of the catalyst over time during the reaction to elucidate the mechanism leading to the excellent properties of EDTA-CQDs (Fig. 10 ). With the increase of illumination time, three new strong signals appear in both EDTA-CQDs and CQDs systems. The peak at 1088 cm−1 represents the formation of ·O2 − [64,77,78], which identifies that the electron transfer occurs between the catalyst and O2 in a two-step single-electron pathway. The stronger signal of EDTA-CQDs indicates a faster charge transfer, which is more than twice that of CQDs system. The other two peaks are attributed to the vibration caused by proton migration in the catalyst structure [61], which may correspond to intermediate products formed by proton donor deprotonation, and their relative changes indirectly demonstrate the conversion of C-OH to CO. During the photocatalytic reaction of CQDs, it can be seen that while the signal attenuation occurs at 1062 cm−1, the signal growth at 1045 cm−1 is synchronous, which is consistent with the trend of C-OH deprotonation to form CO on the surface of CQDs. As a proton donor, the transformation of C-OH affects the growth kinetics of these two peaks. This phenomenon also exists in the EDTA-CQDs system, and the variation is more significant, demonstrating the stronger proton utilization performance of EDTA-CQDs. The observation of proton migration on the catalyst surface confirms that the proton supply of CQDs is relatively low, while the formed proton relay on EDTA-CQDs photocatalyst effectively promotes the proton transfer.

When analyzing the reasons for the high PHP activity of functionalized CQDs, it is worth noting that in addition to the protons on CQDs, EDTA-CQDs can also utilize protons in water to complete the ORR. Detection of pH during the reaction process (Table 1) reveals that an acidic environment is more conducive to the generation of H2O2 by EDTA-CQDs. As the pH value decreases, the H2O2 generation rate in EDTA-CQDs system significantly increases, reaching 2350 μmol g−1h−1 at pH 3, which is 2.9 times higher than at pH 7. After 1 h of reaction, the pH values of system increase, indicating that the amide bond of EDTA-CQDs can also obtain proton from the solution and serve as a proton relay in the ORR. Transferring proton from water to the catalyst surface can further increase local proton concentration to participate in the catalytic reaction. This is an effect that CQDs cannot achieve. The pH of the CQDs system remains almost unchanged after the reaction, that is, the –COOH group on the catalyst is not deprotonated [79]. Reducing the pH value does not enhance the production of H2O, indicating that increasing the proton concentration in the solution cannot improve the photocatalytic activity. The H2O2 yield of EDTA-CQDs in a neutral environment still reaches 890 μmol g−1h−1, which proves the proton supply capacity of amide bond itself. In addition, the increase in pH of EDTA-CQDs system after the reaction suggests that protons on –COOH may transfer to proton relay to react with intermediates of O2 reduction, and then be replenished by protons in water. The amino group on EDTA has lone pair electrons, which can attract the protons of carbonxyl group connected to it and transfer them to the proton relay. In addition, due to the conjugation stabilization of amino group, the carboxyl group attached to the amino group in EDTA is more acidic and easier to transfer protons [80]. Therefore, the carboxyl group in the modifier plays a role in the proton transport in water. In contrast, carboxyl group attached to carbon atom in CQDs lacks this stabilizing effect, has lower acidity and relatively weaker proton transfer ability.

Based on the above results, the possible mechanism for the involvement of proton relay on the surface of EDTA-CQDs in the photocatalytic reactions was proposed (Fig. 11 ) and compared with CQDs [81–83]. CQDs have appropriate band positions and bandgap energies, enabling them to be photoexcited to generate electrons and holes. The electrons generated by photoexcitation transfer to the surface of CQDs to reduce O2, and then react with the proton provided by hydroxyl groups to generate H2O2. The hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs are converted to carbonyl groups. For EDTA-CQDs, in addition to the active functional group (hydroxyl groups) on the catalyst itself and the formed amide bonds providing protons, the amide bonds can also promote intramolecular proton transfer and obtain protons from the solution, acting as a proton relay to promote the PCET reaction. Overall, the connection between EDTA and CQDs plays a role of proton relay through amide bonds. Sufficient proton supply ensures effective electron transfer and O2 utilization, promoting the H2O2 production through PCET reaction.

In this study, an efficient carbon-based metal-free photocatalyst was developed, which exhibited outstanding photocatalytic activity in the absence of sacrificial agent, making it suitable for clean production of H2O2. Experiments demonstrated that EDTA molecules were successfully connected to CQDs through the amide bond, which served as a proton relay with the ability to activate and transfer protons. The accelerated proton transfer promotes electron transmission through the PCET process, leading to an increase in H2O2 production rate via continuous single electron ORR pathway. This research confirmed that designing and constructing proton relay on non-metallic photocatalyst can promote the transfer and utilization of charges within the system, providing a creative new direction for improving electron utilization and reaction rates in photocatalytic reactions. In addition, this study is of great significance for the development of ORR systems that are highly dependent on the oxygen content in water, making continuous photocatalysis possible without the need for aeration to maintain dissolved oxygen levels.
